speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Klaristenfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you guys see where I put my purse?

speaker: interviewer
text: Roll 1, Renee Klaristenfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: --looking for my purse. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's under the pictures, here, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, here it is. Yeah-- here.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Today, February 15, 1995. The testimony of Renee Klaristenfeld. The interviewer, Sandy Jacobson. City and state-- Los Angeles, California, United States. It will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Renee, thank you very much for participating in the project. Please tell me your name and when you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: My married name?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, Renee Klaristenfeld. I was born January 26, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Kiss-- K-I-S-S. The Hebrew name was Rivka. And at Romanian school, where I started, it was Rosalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sighet, Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then can you tell me about your family? Well, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my mother, my father, my grandmother lived in the same city. My mother was born in that city. I had two brothers, and we were four girls, so three sisters. I was the youngest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know what you want me to tell you. How old was your oldest sibling-- how much older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- I would say about 22 years older. Yes. She was married. She must have been married before I was born, because her oldest child was just almost the same age as I-- I was. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then I had two brothers. Then we were three girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother and father. Your mother became a grandmother at the same time she became a mother, in your case.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was a little bit older.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know their ages. I-- I would say approximately-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother may have been around 40, 41, when I was born. And because I don't know whether I recall-- it's in the back of my mind that they were in their 50s when we left. She was probably around 54, maybe 55. I don't know. They never discussed their age.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did your father do? Well, my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my grandparents had first a-- like, a shirt factory. And they were quite successful. There was a sign up on a tall building. Not as tall as the Empire State Building, but-- with their name on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father, when he married my mother, wanted to join in the business. My grandfather insisted that he sit and learn the Torah for 10 years. And my mother was very beautiful. Even when they took us away, she was still a very beautiful woman. And so I guess he was anxious enough to marry her, and he agreed to do that. And then, later, he joined.

speaker: interviewer
text: He went into the family business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work in the business at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never worked. Never-- a very pampered lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the house you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [BACKGROUND NOISE] What happened, actually, is the house was a-- brings back memories, later on, when I was already a young teenager. It has a little bit of a story to it. It was a beautiful house. It had a big courtyard. And it had, like, a building on the right side and on the left side. What happened, though, was that when my grandfather died, at that point they were in the textile business. When my grandfather died-- and I guess the business wasn't as successful, or they couldn't run it as well, or whatever happened-- that it's obvious they had to give up that home and move into a smaller one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was nothing as exciting. It was a small house. It had a courtyard. It had a huge pear tree that we used to pick when they were ripe. And that's about it. Did you have your own room, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not when-- I was growing up already, and we had moved to the smaller house, I shared with a sister. It was very rare where you had a large family that everybody had a bedroom-- very rare, even when you had a very large house. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother-- my-- my grandmother was always in the business. So my mother [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was always brought up with-- with servants. And, for that reason, she-- she was really-- when it came to the bad times, she was a little bit helpless. She wasn't accustomed to doing things. I remember an incident when we were already in the ghetto, and the maid couldn't come in because there was already no maid service in those days. And my mother spoke to me and said, my goodness, it's going to be Pesach, and how are we going to get the house ready, because she was really just not the type who could clean a house. And I said, well, you have three daughters, and so I'm sure that we will manage. I said, I'll tell one of my sisters [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to wash the floors, and I'll wash the windows. And we'll get the house clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother said, oh my god, the neighbors will see you wash a window. I mean, how-- how can we do that? And I said, well, mother, you have a choice. Either clean windows, or the neighbors can see me. So.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, so I remember that times went from good to worse, you know. But we were comfortable. We were always very nicely dressed. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember-- tell me about your family's religious observances.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were brought up-- the children were brought up in a very Hasidic home. My father had a beard-- had a long beard. My mother always used to say, you children didn't see your father when he was very handsome, without the beard. But she used to beg him-- maybe you can cut a little. But it's against the very Orthodox rule to cut your beard, and so. He was very intelligent. He was very educated. People used to come to him-- he had magnificent handwriting. People used to come to them if they needed legal letters and such.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those are the things I remember. But it was a-- I was really very attached to my mother. I think that my father was so immersed in his studies. It was one of his great pleasures. I had a brother who took after him that was really a giant scholar.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I guess, me being the little one, and he having reached a certain age, didn't fuss around with me so much. So I don't remember any [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cuddling with the father, you know, but which they probably didn't do-- the very Hasidic people, at any rate. With my mother, I was very, very close. I adored her. She used to get dressed Saturday morning to go to shul, and I would beg her to wake me up so that I can walk her. And then I would turn around and come back. But I was so proud of the way she looked, and so I would just take her around and walk with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the holidays? Can you describe-- for example, you talked before about Passover. Can you remember a Passover when you were young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk about it a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I remember it was very beautifully conducted. My father took a lot of time. And you're supposed to explain to your children about the exodus from Egypt. And year after year, we would just sit there, and my father would say it in Hebrew, and then he would translate it in Yiddish. And this would go on and on. And since I don't remember my grandfather, and my grandmother always came for holidays and Saturdays. And every day, actually, she came to visit. But she liked to be independent and live in her own home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember, as a matter of fact, an incident where my grandmother was questioning something. And I said, Grandmother, you have heard it so many years, how come you need to ask again? You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was conducted very beautifully. The table, you know, was very festive. And, of course, you're hustling and bustling to get ready for the holidays, and a very festive mood. And the kids were waiting to get new shoes and new clothes, and, you know, it was a great excitement.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have outsiders, people from outside the family, at your table?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall that. No, I don't recall. We had company on Pesach-- I-- I think it was just the custom to stay-- you know, my sister might have. When I was younger, she might have come with her family. But I think if you got together, it was mostly family, for these important holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about your school. Tell me about the school that you went to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said, I didn't have the opportunity to-- to go on to high school. My father was-- there was the one high school with nuns, conducted. Nuns conducted the high school. He objected, vehemently. I mean, he didn't even like the idea I was going to a movie. But my mother used to sneak us in sometimes. We used to read books, for instance. And very often, if he came into the room, we would hide it under the pillow, because he was, at that point, so thoroughly Hasidic and so observant that he didn't-- he didn't like us to read Hungarian books-- romance novels, and so on. But we did. We managed to do it. And with the schooling, really it was like, I cannot say that I reached a great education at home, but right after the war, when I came to Cluj, I went back for a while, there. I had an English professor who gave me courses in English. And when I came to the United States, I went to night school. But then I got married and had the children, and so it took quite a few years til I went to college.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in public school, as a child, were you with-- did you actually go to a public school with other children from your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, everybody, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish and Jewish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk a little bit about the relationship that you had, first of all, that you as a kid had with other children-- non-Jewish children. Were those kids anti-Semitic, or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the ones I associated with. I had-- there was a little girl I remember who was non-Jewish. I was very good friends with her. She didn't come to my home. I don't know how my father would react to it. But my-- but I would go every now and then to hers. I knew that I cannot eat there. If they offered me any food, I would say, well, I can't, because I'm kosher, you know. But I had one or two friends. But otherwise, you were mostly with your own circle of people-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how about in the town itself? Was there anti-Semitism extant in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot of talk of anti-Semitism. I don't recall anybody throwing a rock in our window, but I remember my mother worrying when my father would come home from shul on a Saturday, because he walked with the long beard. And-- and you heard incidences where young, you know, Gentile boys would throw rocks or would-- you know, it wasn't extreme. It wasn't like a pogrom, where you were worried about, you know, having your whole family killed, at least not in my time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, as far as you know, there were never any incidents toward your father, though your mother was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Towards my father, I'm not aware, no. But you heard of incidences, and you knew that anti-Semitism exists.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a town was your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were about, uh-- I believe there were about 50,000 people, and there were about 12,000 to 15,000 Jewish people. It was very-- a lot of Jewish people. As a matter of fact, I looked up in the and it tells you that the first settlers were there in the 1700s, you know. Very slowly they trickled in, and it became like a very nice, nice heimische town. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that, in your home, your mother would speak-- you would speak in Yiddish. What other languages were spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that the language that was spoken in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because the parents were born in Hungary. As a matter of fact, my mother never learned Romanian, because she just spoke Hungarian. And when we went to-- to Romanian school, the teachers would threaten us, because the students were always speaking Hungarian to each other. And they just wanted you to speak Romanian. But there was a custom, really, to speak mostly Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because it had been a Hungarian city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So, now, about when did you become aware that there was trouble brewing and that there was a war? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, since I was young, and I wasn't very involved in politics, or interested in it, but what I overheard would have been, let's say, about a year or two earlier before they took us away, in 1944, because they listened to radio, and they heard, and people came from Poland. Now, when Hitler went into Poland, a lot of the people escaped to Hungary, hoping to escape-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew that there is a lot of problems, that people are in ghettos. And then I remember hearing that people are being taken away, and that they are hungry, and that they are-- I mean, terrible stories that I overheard. And I know that my mother was absolutely very worried.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember my father expressing concern. I'm sure he was. But, being the almost holy man that he was, I guess he just relied on the fact that God will help us.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother had a brother here who came in 1938. And she kept saying to my father, why don't we pick up and go to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I recall hearing my father say, I cannot do that, because my children will become, quote-unquote, 'shkotzim and shiksas.' Have to translate? Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. A 'shegetz' is a Gentile boy, and a 'shiksa' is a Gentile girl. And he didn't mean to say anything against the Gentiles. What he wanted to say is that we would forget about our religion, and we will get assimilated. And so he rather took his chances. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then-- so what happened, actually, like, in the late '30s and early '40s? Had there been a shift in your-- in the city government or-- your city was Romanian, but did it stay Romanian?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Romanian until 1940. And then it became Hungary. I wasn't aware of any particular shift. I-- as far as I recall, I don't know. Like I said, I don't know what was going on. I know we went on with our lives as usual, until we heard these terrible stories happening in Poland, after Hitler went into Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and so I guess my mother was living in dread and-- and fearing the worst, you know? And that went on for a while, until it came true. All right. And what is your memory of the time, the day, that it came true-- the day that the worst happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first we were in the ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you moved into a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were moved into the ghetto-- I couldn't tell you exactly. I would say it was two month earlier, three month earlier. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I'm sorry-- earlier that what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Earlier than they took us away. In other words, they took us away in, um-- I think it was May--

speaker: interviewee
text: --of 1944. And prior to that, it must have been about two, three months that the Germans came into town. And we knew already then that there is what you call a lot of 'tsuris.' And-- and they said we have to be in a ghetto. Now, our house, our street, was fenced in-- part of the ghetto. So we felt very fortunate that we don't have to get out of our own home, because people who lived in very comfortable homes had to move in with families where they got one room for a whole family, and it was very cramped and very uncomfortable. As a matter of fact, if I may say this, I have a memory of a very dear friend that perished in the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Her-- after we had moved out of that beautiful home that I described, my-- a rabbi moved in. A rabbi and his family lived there. And the daughter was my friend-- a very good friend of mine. And they had to move out. They were across the street from us, but our side stayed in the ghetto, and they had to move out. And I remember another friend telling me that she is sick. She is very sick. She was always a very, very pretty, tall, blonde, very slim little girl. And so we went through the backyards, in order to go visit her. And I remember coming in to a very dingy room where she was sleeping on a-- laying on a mattress. And I remember her mother being so grateful that we came to see her and saying that she  is sick. And that was the last I saw her. And then I found out that she never made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But do you know what she died of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have no idea, because if-- if you didn't get killed, you could have died either of hunger, or of torture, or-- or just giving up. I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you describe the ghetto? Let's start with the outside of the ghetto. Was it very, very large?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto, itself?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Were there-- was it-- how many blocks, would you say? Can you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. That's why I told you, interviewing me may not be that interesting, because I don't recall, really, it-- I was confined, because they wouldn't let us out, at that point, too much from the house. The Germans were all over. They tried to be very friendly, especially if they saw a young girl. My parents were petrified to let us out of the house. And so, like I said, we were just fortunate to stay in our home as long as we did. And we were not inconvenienced as much. We couldn't get out in the street. That was not part of the ghetto. I remember I-- they always said that I look like a German, because I had very light blonde hair. And just before the ghetto-- this is an interesting incident-- when my friend, the rabbi, still lived across the street. I remember the Germans were there, and they moved the rabbi into one building. And they took over the other building. And there was a guard at the gate. There was a big gate. And I decided to-- everybody told me you don't look Jewish. So I said, well, I'll just make myself free and go out whenever I feel like it. And I went to visit this friend. And I remember-- though that was actually not in the ghetto yet. That was just before the ghetto. I decided to go visit her.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the Germans were around, and this guy asked me-- this German soldier-- asked me, where are you going? And I said, I'm going to visit my friend. And he said, well, why would you want to visit a Jewish girl, you know? And I guess I was afraid to tell him that I am, too, so I just said, well, I like her, and so I want to visit her, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when we were in the ghetto already, they were hanging around and took over. There was a building in the back of the courtyard with a mother and a daughter. And they gave them one room, and they took over the rest of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were always around and always trying to make conversation, and, uh-- you know. But they were-- I don't remember them being rude or coming into our house. But they were everywhere, and so that was enough to feel uncomfortable. In the ghetto you talked about people having to move into other houses. Did anybody come to move into your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't, because I guess there wasn't that much room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of whether or not the ghetto contained only the members-- I mean, the people from your own community, or were they bringing Jews in from the countryside, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, definitely our community, because by the time they came to our city, there-- every other city was already taken over by the Germans. And-- and they took-- excuse me. They took everybody from their own homes. Every city had their own ghetto, and they took them from their own homes. The only thing was, they-- they-- I think I will take a little bit of this. [DRINKS] They-- some Polish, from Poland-- like I said, there was some people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this-- in the ghetto? --that the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that they had come from-- they had escaped from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had come to escape, and they probably wound up. But what happened was, sometime-- when they started-- before they started to take away the Jews, they took away people who didn't have Hungarian citizenship. So the Polish people were taken away first. My father had a sister who lived in the city, with her husband and her grown daughter in a very lovely little house that was always, I remember, so spotless. And we heard that they were going to take them away the next day because the husband was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, at that point, we were not told yet anything about being taken away. But I was told in the evening that they're going to take them away tomorrow. And so I remember getting up very, very early in the morning and running to their house, just to see them before they leave. And I remember looking in the door and seeing a spotless kitchen-- everything just perfectly left. And they were gone. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were gone because he was Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so, now, in the ghetto-- and you're in your own little house-- how did you get food? Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't remember. I didn't do the shopping. I don't know how it got to the house. I would imagine that they allowed you certain hours to go out and shop. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't remember whether there was currency? Do you remember who, um--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the currency was the Hungarian currency. No, that didn't change.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't change that. Do you remember if there was Jewish administration of the ghetto? Did they discuss that-- if they had set up a Jewish committee to govern the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not aware of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were in the ghetto a couple of months, you recall. And it was your whole family. How about your sister-- your married sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in her own city, which was quite a distance from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so she didn't live in the same town.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in the ghetto a couple of months. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they just started rounding up the Jews to take them away. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I need a minute. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so they-- they took some-- they took groups. These streets, they'll take today, and then-- I remember they took away, maybe for two days, some Jews, and then it was our turn. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they lined us up in the street. And there was an elderly man who was, like, a very important man in town-- very aristocratic family. And was really somebody very special-- a very important man. He was related to my grandmother. And I remember they lined us up, and they allowed us one little bundle, whatever, to take along with you. And, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were standing in line. And, um-- I don't know. This man stepped out of line for a minute, and this German guy went over-- a young German-- and gave him a shove. I thought this old man is going to fly, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was totally incensed. I was so angry, because I felt that it was so unjustified, you know. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my way of thinking was always, if-- if you don't provoke, and if you don't-- you know, if you just go about your business-- you know, that it's very unjust. And so when he got done, he walked over to me and started talking to me. I guess, being that I looked so German. And I was young. And-- and I remember saying to him, in German, I don't want to talk to you.
